markets.question: Will the Fed Cut–Pause–Pause in the next three decisions (Apr–Jun–Jul)?
markets.description:
The FED interest rates are defined in this market by the upper bound of the target federal funds rate. The decisions on the target federal funds rate are made by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meetings.

This market will resolve according to the decisions made by the next three Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meetings: April 28-29; June 16-17; and July 28-29.

A qualifying cut occurs when the new upper bound of the target federal funds rate is lower compared to the level it was prior to the respective meeting.

A qualifying hike occurs when the new upper bound of the target federal funds rate is higher compared to the level it was prior to the respective meeting.

A qualifying pause occurs when the new upper bound of the target federal funds rate is equal to the level it was prior to the respective meeting.

If the Fed publishes a different combination than any listed, this market will resolve to "Other". Any rate hike will be encompassed by "Other".

Emergency rate cuts outside the regularly scheduled meetings will not be considered.

The resolution source for this market is the FOMC’s statement after its meetings:
https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/fomccalendars.htm

The level and change of the target federal funds rate is also published at the official website of the Federal Reserve:
https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/openmarket.htm

events.title: Fed decisions (Apr-Jul)
events.description:

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket prices an 78.5% implied probability for the Federal Reserve to pause interest rates at the April 28-29, June 16-17, and July 28-29 FOMC meetings, reflecting sticky inflation and a resilient labor market amid geopolitical oil shocks. March 2026 CPI surged 3.3% year-over-year—driven by energy prices amid Iran tensions—with core at 2.6%, while nonfarm payrolls added a stronger-than-expected 178,000 jobs and unemployment held at 4.3%. The Fed maintained its 3.50%-3.75% target range at the March 17-18 meeting, signaling one cut later in 2026. CME FedWatch aligns, showing >98% April hold odds and elevated pause probabilities through summer, with April CPI (due May 12) as the next key catalyst.

markets.question: Stefan Dostanic vs. Federico Agustin Gomez: Total Sets O/U 2.5
markets.description:
This market refers to the tennis match between Stefan Dostanic and Federico Agustin Gomez in the Savannah, originally scheduled for April 20, 2026 at 10:00AM ET.
This market will resolve to "Over" if the total number of sets completed equals or exceeds 3. Otherwise, it will resolve to "Under."
A super tie breaker is considered as one (1) set for total set markets.
If the match begins but is not completed, this market will resolve 50-50. If the match is canceled before play begins or delayed beyond 7 days from the scheduled date without a result, this market will also resolve 50-50.
Resolution will be based on official Challenger statistics.
markets.groupItemTitle: Savannah: Stefan Dostanic vs Federico Agustin Gomez Total Sets: O/U 2.5

events.title: Savannah: Stefan Dostanic vs Federico Agustin Gomez
events.description:
This market refers to the tennis match between Stefan Dostanic and Federico Agustin Gomez in the Savannah, originally scheduled for April 20, 2026 at 10:00AM ET.

This market will resolve to 'Stefan Dostanic' if Stefan Dostanic advances against Federico Agustin Gomez.

This market will resolve to 'Federico Agustin Gomez' if Federico Agustin Gomez advances against Stefan Dostanic.

If the match is canceled (not played at all), ends in a tie, or is delayed beyond 7 days from the scheduled date without a winner determined, this market will resolve to 50-50.

If the match begins but is not completed, and one player advances due to the opponent's retirement, default, or disqualification, this market will resolve to the player who advances.

If the match ends in a walkover (player withdraws before the start and the other advances automatically), this market will resolve to 50-50.

The primary resolution source will be official information from the ATP Tour. A consensus of credible reporting may also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: In the Savannah Challenger's first round on green clay, No. 167 Federico Agustin Gomez faces No. 268 Stefan Dostanic in their head-to-head debut, with trader consensus reflecting a closely contested matchup. Gomez, an Argentine clay veteran, posted solid recent U.S. clay results including a Tallahassee semifinal run and Sarasota quarterfinal, but Dostanic counters with momentum from reaching the Sarasota final last week despite an early Tallahassee exit. No injuries reported from official updates; Dostanic gains home-crowd edge and familiarity with Har-Tru green clay conditions. Recent qualifying concluded without disruptions, setting up a stylistic battle favoring baseline grinders amid neutral weather.

markets.question: Will 4 Fed rate cuts happen in 2026?
markets.description:
This market will resolve according to the exact amount of cuts of 25 basis points in 2026 by the Fed (including any cuts made during the December meeting).

Emergency rate cuts outside of scheduled FOMC meetings will also count toward the total number of cuts in 2026. This market will remain open until December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, to account for any such emergency actions.

For example, if the Fed cuts rates by 50 bps after a meeting, it would be considered 2 cuts (of 25 bps each).

This market will resolve early to "No" if the specified number of cuts becomes impossible — i.e., if more cuts have already occurred than the strike in question.

Note that cuts between 1–24 bps (inclusive) will also be considered 1 rate cut.

The resolution source for this market will be FOMC statements after meetings scheduled in 2026 according to the official calendar: https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/fomccalendars.htm. The level and change of the target federal funds rate is also published at the official website of the Federal Reserve at https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/openmarket.htm.

eventMetadata.context_description: Polymarket traders' closely contested implied probabilities—34% for zero Fed rate cuts (0 basis points) and 29.5% for one (25 basis points) in 2026—stem from March FOMC minutes released April 8, which highlighted upside inflation risks from a Middle East oil shock pushing Brent crude above $100 per barrel and core PCE to 3.0-3.1%. March CPI surged 0.9% monthly to 3.3% yearly, offsetting a stable 4.3% unemployment rate and modest job gains. While the Fed's March dot plot median projects fed funds at 2.4% by year-end (versus today's 3.50%-3.75% range), trader consensus prices persistent disinflation hurdles, with two-sided risks including potential hikes. Key differentiator: transitory oil effects could enable one cut; prolongation favors none. Watch April 28-29 FOMC for signals.

markets.question: Will Trump end Department of Education before 2027?
markets.description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if the US Department of Education ceases operations entirely, including the termination of all federal educational programs managed by the department, by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to "No".

If the US Department of Education is merged with another agency, resulting in a consolidated department with a shared administrative structure which is no longer titled the Department of Education it will count as a "Yes" resolution.

If it becomes impossible for Trump to sign legislation/perform executive actions (e.g. he resigns), this market will resolve to "No".

The primary resolution source for this market will be official information from the US government, however a consensus of credible reporting will also be used.

events.title: Will Trump end Department of Education before 2027?
events.description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if the US Department of Education ceases operations entirely, including the termination of all federal educational programs managed by the department, by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to "No".

If the US Department of Education is merged with another agency, resulting in a consolidated department with a shared administrative structure which is no longer titled the Department of Education it will count as a "Yes" resolution.

If it becomes impossible for Trump to sign legislation/perform executive actions (e.g. he resigns), this market will resolve to "No".

The primary resolution source for this market will be official information from the US government, however a consensus of credible reporting will also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus reflects near-certainty at 96.7% that President Trump will not fully eliminate the Department of Education before 2027, as abolishing the cabinet agency requires congressional legislation amid stalled progress on H.R. 899, introduced in January 2025 to terminate it by December 31, 2026. Executive orders in March 2025 and November 2025 initiated restructuring by shifting grants and functions to states and other agencies under Secretary Linda McMahon, but Congress rejected defunding requests in the February 2026 spending law, preserving appropriations. With eight months remaining before the deadline and midterm elections looming, key Republican holdouts favor reform over outright elimination, facing filibuster hurdles without 60 Senate votes or successful reconciliation. Late-breaking scandals, unified GOP supermajorities post-midterms, or expedited budget maneuvers could marginally shift odds, though structural barriers remain formidable.

markets.question: Will the Republican Party win the OH-01 House seat?
markets.description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the OH-01 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democratic Rep. Greg Landsman's OH-01 seat strengthened as leading forecasters like Cook Political Report shifted the race from Toss Up to Lean Democrat earlier this month, citing his fundraising edge and incumbency advantages in the newly redrawn R+5 district. This adjustment, alongside Inside Elections' similar move, reflects trader consensus favoring a Democratic hold despite the 2025 Ohio GOP gerrymander adding rural Warren and Clinton counties. Landsman faces a May 5 Democratic primary challenge from Damon Lynch IV over policy differences, while Republicans hold a fragmented four-way primary among newcomers Holly Adams, Eric Conroy, Steven Erbeck, and Rosemary Oglesby-Henry, potentially yielding a weaker general election nominee on November 3. Absent public polling, these ratings shifts drive the market's Democratic tilt.

markets.question: Law banning sports prediction markets enacted in 2026?
markets.description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if any bill with the effect of banning CFTC-regulated prediction markets from offering sports betting contracts is signed into law by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to "No".

Any bill that prohibits federally regulated prediction markets from offering sports betting contracts, or otherwise places such activities under state-level gambling regulation rather than federal regulatory oversight, will qualify for a “Yes” resolution.

Qualifying legislation may include joint resolutions and must pass both the House and the Senate, and must be signed by the President, become law without signature while Congress remains in session, or become law through veto override. Presidential pocket vetoes that expire resolve to "No".

The primary resolution source for this market will be official information from the US federal government and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC); however, a consensus of credible reporting will also be used.

events.title: Law banning sports prediction markets enacted in 2026?
events.description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if any bill with the effect of banning CFTC-regulated prediction markets from offering sports betting contracts is signed into law by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to "No".

Any bill that prohibits federally regulated prediction markets from offering sports betting contracts, or otherwise places such activities under state-level gambling regulation rather than federal regulatory oversight, will qualify for a “Yes” resolution.

Qualifying legislation may include joint resolutions and must pass both the House and the Senate, and must be signed by the President, become law without signature while Congress remains in session, or become law through veto override. Presidential pocket vetoes that expire resolve to "No".

The primary resolution source for this market will be official information from the US federal government and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC); however, a consensus of credible reporting will also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: Bipartisan legislation introduced in March 2026, led by Sens. Adam Schiff and John Curtis via the Prediction Markets Are Gambling Act (S.4160), aims to amend the Commodity Exchange Act and bar CFTC-registered platforms like Kalshi and Polymarket from listing sports event contracts akin to gambling. Similar bills, including Rep. Jamie Raskin's STOP Corrupt Bets Act, target broader restrictions but remain stalled at introduction with no committee hearings or votes scheduled amid congressional gridlock. The CFTC's March advisory and advance notice of proposed rulemaking prioritize regulatory frameworks over outright bans. Minnesota's state-level prohibition advanced past initial committees in early April but faces hurdles. This lack of momentum drives the 89.5% "No" trader consensus, though late-year scandals or expedited votes could shift odds.

eventMetadata.context_description: Evanston Mayor Daniel Biss secured the Democratic nomination in IL-09's March 17 primary following Rep. Jan Schakowsky's 2025 retirement announcement, facing pastor John Elleson after the Republican primary. This solidly Democratic district (Cook PVI D+19) has delivered consistent large margins for Democrats, with no Republican hold since the 1940s, driving trader consensus to 92.5% for the Democratic Party ahead of the November 3 general election. The commanding position reflects Biss's local profile, weak GOP infrastructure in this Chicago-north-suburban seat, and absent early general polling. Upsets would require a Biss scandal, extraordinary GOP midterm wave, or depressed Democratic turnout.

eventMetadata.context_description: The open race in California's 26th Congressional District following incumbent Democrat Julia Brownley's January 2026 retirement announcement has not shifted trader consensus, with odds reflecting the district's D+8 partisan lean and consistent Democratic general election margins of 12-21 points since 2020. Five Democrats, led by state Sen. Jacqui Irwin with nearly $500,000 cash on hand as of late March, dominate the June 2 top-two primary field over four Republicans, positioning a Democrat to advance and secure victory in November. Ratings from Cook Political Report (Solid Democratic) and others reinforce this outlook. Potential disruptions include a surprise GOP top-two sweep, nominee scandal, or strong Republican national midterm tailwinds.

markets.question: Ukraine signs peace deal with Russia by June 30?
markets.description:
This market will resolve to “Yes”  if, Ukraine signs any written instrument (e.g., treaty, ceasefire/armistice, framework/“roadmap,” exchange of letters, or mediated agreement text) that: (i) includes both Ukraine and the Russian Federation as parties, and (ii) either ends hostilities/establishes a ceasefire or commits both sides to a defined process toward ending the war (i.e., stated objective of peace/normalization plus principles, steps, and/or a timetable) by June 30, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to “No”. 

Only Ukraine’s signature is required; Russia’s signature or ratification is not.

Localized, temporary, or issue-specific arrangements—such as airstrike-limitation or deconfliction protocols, humanitarian pauses, evacuation corridors, prisoner-exchange or trade/export arrangements, border/DMZ adjustments, or ceasefires limited to a particular sector/front/municipality—will not qualify.

The document must bear a wet-ink or officially issued electronic signature of an authorized Ukrainian representative. Unsigned agreements (e.g., the 2023 Ohrid arrangement) will not qualify regardless of if they are otherwise officially enacted. 

The primary resolution source will be a consensus of credible reporting.

events.description:
This market will resolve to “Yes”  if, Ukraine signs any written instrument (e.g., treaty, ceasefire/armistice, framework/“roadmap,” exchange of letters, or mediated agreement text) that: (i) includes both Ukraine and the Russian Federation as parties, and (ii) either ends hostilities/establishes a ceasefire or commits both sides to a defined process toward ending the war (i.e., stated objective of peace/normalization plus principles, steps, and/or a timetable) by June 30, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to “No”. 

Only Ukraine’s signature is required; Russia’s signature or ratification is not.

Localized, temporary, or issue-specific arrangements—such as airstrike-limitation or deconfliction protocols, humanitarian pauses, evacuation corridors, prisoner-exchange or trade/export arrangements, border/DMZ adjustments, or ceasefires limited to a particular sector/front/municipality—will not qualify.

The document must bear a wet-ink or officially issued electronic signature of an authorized Ukrainian representative. Unsigned agreements (e.g., the 2023 Ohrid arrangement) will not qualify regardless of if they are otherwise officially enacted. 

The primary resolution source will be a consensus of credible reporting.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus prices "No" at 94.5% for a Ukraine-Russia peace deal by June 30, reflecting stalled diplomatic efforts amid irreconcilable demands over territory, security guarantees, and Donbas recognition. Recent mutual accusations of violating an Orthodox Easter ceasefire on April 9-10 underscore persistent distrust, with no resumption of substantive talks since the Iran conflict diverted focus in March. Russian Foreign Minister Lavrov stated three days ago that negotiations are not a priority, while Ukrainian President Zelenskyy anticipates pressure through summer without breakthroughs. Ongoing frontline stalemate and Russia's firm conditions present major barriers, though a sudden trilateral summit or U.S.-brokered concessions could shift odds before the deadline.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Rep. Mike Flood's unopposed Republican primary on May 12 and dominant fundraising—$1.4 million cash on hand versus $209,000 for leading Democrat Chris Backemeyer—anchor trader consensus at 78.5% for a Republican hold in the R+6 NE-01 district. Forecasters like Cook Political Report rate it Solid Republican, citing Flood's prior general election margins of 60% in 2024 and 58% in 2022 despite Lincoln's Democratic lean. Recent local coverage highlights Backemeyer and Eric Moyer's door-knocking ahead of their May 12 primary, but no polling emerges to signal competitiveness, with rural turnout dynamics key to any upset. Odds reflect incumbency edge and historical base rates in safe House seats.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Rep. Melanie Stansbury's strong reelection bid in New Mexico's 1st Congressional District drives trader consensus at 92% for Democrats, anchored by the district's D+7 partisan voter index and her history of double-digit general election margins—56%-44% in 2024 and 55%-44% in 2022. With primaries on June 2, Stansbury faces no Democratic challengers, while Republican Ndidiamaka Okpareke holds just $30,000 cash-on-hand versus Stansbury's $356,000 as of late March, underscoring fundraising disparities in this Solid Democratic-rated seat per Cook Political Report. Potential shifts could arise from late scandals, health issues, or a national Republican midterm wave boosting turnout in battleground areas like Albuquerque.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Rep. Chrissy Houlahan's commanding trader consensus at 92.5% for Democrats in Pennsylvania's 6th Congressional District stems from the district's D+6 partisan lean, her history of double-digit general election victories—including 56% in 2024—and overwhelming fundraising superiority with $3.9 million cash on hand versus Republican Marty Young's $176,000 as of late March. Forecasters like Cook Political Report rate it Safe Democratic, reflecting Chester County's reliable Democratic suburban turnout. With unopposed primaries on May 19, no recent catalysts have shifted sentiment. Late-breaking scandals, health issues, legal developments, or a national Republican midterm wave could narrow odds, though structural advantages make an upset unlikely.

markets.question: Will the Republican Party win the WI-06 House seat?
markets.description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the WI-06 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Glenn Grothman’s commanding position in the R+8 Wisconsin 6th Congressional District, where he secured 61% in 2024 amid Donald Trump’s 58% district margin, drives trader consensus to 83.5% for a Republican victory. Massive fundraising disparities—Grothman’s $700,000 cash on hand dwarfs the fragmented eight-candidate Democratic primary field’s totals under $200,000 combined—bolster his edge ahead of the August 11 primaries. Independent challenger Mike Thurow’s January launch and $24,000 cash highlight a split opposition, while ratings from Cook Political Report (Solid Republican, updated January 2026) and others affirm the race’s safety despite national House generic ballot trends favoring Democrats. No polls exist, but structural factors favor the GOP hold barring a major wave.

markets.question: Les Wexner charged by June 30?
markets.description:
This market will resolve to “Yes” if any Federal or State jurisdiction of the United States formally charges or otherwise announces a criminal indictment of Leslie Wexner between market creation and June 30, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to “No”.

For the purposes of this market the District of Columbia and any county, municipality, or other subdivision of a State shall be included within the definition of a State.

The primary resolution source for this market will be official information from US governmental sources, however a wide consensus of credible reporting will also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket heavily favors no criminal charges against Les Wexner by June 30, driven by the absence of any DOJ indictments or federal announcements despite intensified scrutiny. In February 2026, the House Oversight Committee deposed Wexner on his financial ties to Jeffrey Epstein, where he denied knowledge of crimes and stated he severed contact nearly two decades ago upon learning of Epstein's abuses; video footage and released DOJ documents underscored past FBI interest but yielded no prosecutions. Civil lawsuits filed by Epstein victims in early April allege Wexner enabled trafficking through funding and property, yet these remain private actions without triggering criminal proceedings. Late-breaking DOJ developments or new evidence could shift odds, but current pricing reflects institutional inaction.

markets.question: Will 7 Fed rate cuts happen in 2026?
markets.description:
This market will resolve according to the exact amount of cuts of 25 basis points in 2026 by the Fed (including any cuts made during the December meeting).

Emergency rate cuts outside of scheduled FOMC meetings will also count toward the total number of cuts in 2026. This market will remain open until December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, to account for any such emergency actions.

For example, if the Fed cuts rates by 50 bps after a meeting, it would be considered 2 cuts (of 25 bps each).

This market will resolve early to "No" if the specified number of cuts becomes impossible — i.e., if more cuts have already occurred than the strike in question.

Note that cuts between 1–24 bps (inclusive) will also be considered 1 rate cut.

The resolution source for this market will be FOMC statements after meetings scheduled in 2026 according to the official calendar: https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/fomccalendars.htm. The level and change of the target federal funds rate is also published at the official website of the Federal Reserve at https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/openmarket.htm.

eventMetadata.context_description: Polymarket traders' closely contested implied probabilities—34% for zero Fed rate cuts (0 basis points) and 29.5% for one (25 basis points) in 2026—stem from March FOMC minutes released April 8, which highlighted upside inflation risks from a Middle East oil shock pushing Brent crude above $100 per barrel and core PCE to 3.0-3.1%. March CPI surged 0.9% monthly to 3.3% yearly, offsetting a stable 4.3% unemployment rate and modest job gains. While the Fed's March dot plot median projects fed funds at 2.4% by year-end (versus today's 3.50%-3.75% range), trader consensus prices persistent disinflation hurdles, with two-sided risks including potential hikes. Key differentiator: transitory oil effects could enable one cut; prolongation favors none. Watch April 28-29 FOMC for signals.

markets.question: Nothing Ever Happens: Obama
markets.description:
This market will resolve to “Something” if any of the following conditions are met between market creation and December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET:

- Obama federally charged
- Obama arrested
- Obama divorce

Otherwise, this market will resolve to “Nothing”.

The full rules for this market can be found here: 
https://polymarket-upload.s3.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/NEH_Obama.pdf

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket prices "Nothing" at 83% for the "Nothing Ever Happens: Obama" market, reflecting the absence of any official federal charges, arrest, or divorce announcement involving former President Barack Obama since the market launched on February 11, 2026, with resolution set for December 31, 2026. Recent developments, including Obama's April 19 meeting with New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani at a Bronx child care center, a Virginia campaign pitch on April 20, and an interview alongside ex-Presidents Clinton, Bush, and Biden, remain routine political engagements with no legal or personal drama. Despite lingering partisan claims from 2025 about 2016 election interference, no DOJ actions, indictments, or credible proceedings have materialized, underscoring historical barriers to prosecuting former presidents absent overwhelming evidence.

eventMetadata.context_description: State Rep. La Shawn Ford's victory in the crowded March 17 Democratic primary solidified the party's nominee to succeed retiring Rep. Danny Davis in safely Democratic IL-07, where Davis's endorsement proved decisive amid heavy outside spending. The district's strong partisan lean—evident in Cook PVI ratings around D+33 and historical general election margins over 70 points for Democrats—drives trader consensus to 93.5% for Democratic retention ahead of the November 3 general election against low-profile Republican Chad Koppie. Realistic challenges would demand a seismic shift, such as a major scandal engulfing Ford, anomalously low Democratic turnout in Chicago's West and South Sides, or an overwhelming national Republican midterm wave.

markets.question: Will the Fed’s lower bound reach 0.5% or lower before 2027?
markets.description:
The FED interest rates are defined in this market by the lower or the upper bound of the target federal funds range. The decisions on the target federal fund range are made by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meetings.

This market will resolve to “Yes” if the lower or the upper bound of the target federal funds rate reaches the specified level at any point by December 31, 2026, 12:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to “No.”

Emergency rate cuts and hikes outside the regularly scheduled meetings will be considered.

The resolution source for this market is the official website of the Federal Reserve at:
https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/openmarket.htm.

This market may resolve as soon as the relevant data showing the reached level is published.

events.title: What will Fed Rate hit before 2027?

The resolution source for this market is the official website of the Federal Reserve at:
https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/openmarket.htm.

This market may resolve as soon as the relevant data showing the reached level is published.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket reflects a steady federal funds target range of 3.50%-3.75%, with the effective rate at 3.64% as of April 2026, amid resilient labor markets showing unemployment dipping to 4.3% in March and surging March CPI inflation at 3.3% year-over-year—driven by gasoline prices—halting near-term easing expectations. The March FOMC dot plot projects one 25-basis-point cut in 2026, targeting 3.00%-3.25% by end-2027, aligning with CME FedWatch probabilities of over 95% for no change at the April 28-29 meeting. Key catalysts ahead include April CPI on May 10 and June FOMC, where persistent inflation above 3% could delay cuts or prompt reassessment of the policy path.

Henry Cuellar's victory in the March 3 Democratic primary with 58% solidified the TX-28 general election matchup against Republican nominee Tano Tijerina, who dominated his primary at 74%, driving trader consensus to price Democrats at 64.5%. Cuellar's edge reflects the district's Democratic lean—evident in Kamala Harris's 61% 2024 presidential win despite R+2 PVI—and ratings from Cook Political Report, Sabato's Crystal Ball, and Inside Elections as Lean or Tilt Democratic, bolstered by Trump's pre-primary pardon resolving Cuellar's legal issues. Tijerina's Trump endorsement and border security focus sustain GOP viability at 34.5% in this South Texas battleground, with competitive Q1 fundraising (Cuellar $764K cash on hand vs. Tijerina $574K); no public polls yet ahead of November 3.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus heavily favors no criminal charges against Bill Gates by June 30, driven by the absence of any official DOJ investigation, indictment announcements, or federal probes into alleged misconduct like Epstein ties or vaccine-related claims. Recent Justice Department Epstein file releases in February prompted House Oversight Committee scrutiny, culminating in Gates' scheduled June 10 testimony alongside others, but no accusations of wrongdoing have materialized—only civil lawsuits abroad, such as a debunked Netherlands vaccine misinformation case misreported as criminal. With under 70 days remaining and no primary indicators of prosecution, traders see insurmountable barriers absent late-breaking evidence or special counsel action, though testimony revelations could theoretically prompt shifts.

markets.question: Will Anthropic provide Mythos to the US government by June 30, 2026?
markets.description:
This market will resolve to “Yes” if Anthropic grants the United States federal government access to Claude Mythos (including Mythos Preview) by the listed date, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to “No”.

Granting access to Claude Mythos refers to Anthropic allowing any department, agency, or subset of the United States federal government to use Claude Mythos for any purpose.

An official announcement from either Anthropic or the United States federal government, that Anthropic has agreed to grant the United States federal government access to Claude Mythos, will be sufficient for a “Yes” resolution, regardless of whether access begins after the resolution date. Suggestions, negotiations, or other statements that do not constitute granting access will not qualify. A consensus of credible reporting that Anthropic has agreed to grant access to Claude Mythos to the United States federal government will also suffice.

“Claude Mythos” refers to a model named “Claude Mythos” (e.g., Claude Mythos Preview, Claude Mythos 1, Claude Mythos X) or a model confirmed by Anthropic or by a consensus of credible reporting to be the same model referenced in the March 26, 2026 leak. Models such as Claude Haiku, Sonnet, Opus 4.7/5.0 or similar will not qualify unless explicitly confirmed to be the same model referenced in the leak.

General public availability of a Claude Mythos model without a qualifying announcement or confirmation of access being granted will not alone suffice.

The primary resolution sources will be official information from Anthropic and the United States federal government, and a consensus of credible reporting.

eventMetadata.context_description: White House efforts to grant federal agencies access to Anthropic's Claude Mythos Preview—a restricted, cybersecurity-focused AI model excelling at vulnerability detection in critical software—have driven recent trader optimism, despite prior Department of War contract failures over Anthropic's ethical safeguards against mass surveillance and autonomous weapons. Labeled a supply chain risk earlier this year, Anthropic is now in productive talks with government officials, with agencies already testing Mythos amid its unmatched cyber defense capabilities outpacing human experts. Limited to vetted partners like Cisco and CrowdStrike due to weaponization fears, provision hinges on resolving legal tensions; watch for contract announcements before June 30, as competitive U.S. AI leadership pressures a deal.

markets.question: Will Stephen Miller leave the Trump administration before 2027?
markets.description:
This market will resolve to “Yes” if it is announced that that the listed individual will leave the Trump Administration, or otherwise ceases to be a member of administration by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to “No”.

An announcement of the listed individual's resignation/removal before this market's end date will immediately resolve this market to "Yes", regardless of when the announced resignation/removal goes into effect.

The Trump administration includes individuals formally appointed by Donald Trump to roles within the U.S. federal government, such as Cabinet members, Executive Office staff, senior policy advisors, ambassadors, or White House staff whose appointments are publicly announced by official government channels.

The primary resolution source will be official information from the Trump administration, however a consensus of credible reopening may also be used.

events.title: Who will leave Trump Administration before 2027?
events.description:
This market will resolve to “Yes” if it is announced that that the listed individual will leave the Trump Administration, or otherwise ceases to be a member of administration by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to “No”.

An announcement of the listed individual's resignation/removal before this market's end date will immediately resolve this market to "Yes", regardless of when the announced resignation/removal goes into effect.

The Trump administration includes individuals formally appointed by Donald Trump to roles within the U.S. federal government, such as Cabinet members, Executive Office staff, senior policy advisors, ambassadors, or White House staff whose appointments are publicly announced by official government channels.

The primary resolution source will be official information from the Trump administration, however a consensus of credible reopening may also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer's resignation on April 20 amid abuse-of-power allegations marks the latest high-profile Cabinet departure from the Trump administration, following Attorney General Pam Bondi's exit on April 3 over mishandling of Jeffrey Epstein files and Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem's in early March amid immigration policy controversies. This recent wave contributes to 32% turnover among top positions as of mid-April, per Brookings tracking, lower than the first term's rate but still elevated. Reports indicate active White House discussions on further changes, including FBI Director Kash Patel and Army Secretary Daniel Driscoll, amid scandals and policy pressures. Traders weigh historical patterns of administration shake-ups against potential late announcements or investigations that could prompt more exits before 2027.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Kelly Morrison's commanding position in Minnesota's 3rd Congressional District, a suburban Minneapolis seat that has shifted safely blue—voting for Kamala Harris by 21 points in 2024—drives trader consensus at 91.5% for the Democratic Party in the 2026 midterm election. Morrison's strong 2024 victory and the district's Cook Political rating as Solid D reflect structural advantages, including favorable demographics and limited GOP infrastructure in this former battleground. With primaries on August 11, no high-profile Republican challengers have emerged, reinforcing the lopsided odds. Realistic challenges could arise from a top-tier GOP recruit, a Democratic scandal, or a national Republican wave amid midterm dynamics, though historical incumbent re-election rates exceed 90% in such districts.

markets.question: Will Republicanos (REPUBLICANOS) win the most seats in the next Brazilian Senate election?
markets.description:
The next federal Senate election is scheduled to take place in Brazil on October 4, 2026, in which two-thirds of the Senate’s 81 seats will be contested.

This market will resolve according to the political party that wins the greatest number of seats in the next Brazilian Senate election. 

Only seats contested in the next Brazilian Senate election will be considered.

If the result of this election isn't known definitively by June 30, 2027, 11:59 PM ET, the market will resolve to "Other".

This market will resolve based on the result of the election, as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the Brazilian government, specifically the Superior Electoral Court (Tribunal Superior Eleitoral, TSE) (e.g., www.tse.jus.br/eleicoes/resultados-eleicoes).

events.title: Next Brazil Senate Election: Most Seats Won

This market will resolve according to the political party that wins the greatest number of seats in the next Brazilian Senate election. 

Only seats contested in the next Brazilian Senate election will be considered.

In the event of a tie between multiple parties for the most seats won, this market will resolve in favor of the party whose listed abbreviation appears first in alphabetical order.

This market's resolution will be based solely on the number of seats won by the named party in the Brazilian Senate, not by any federation or coalition it may be a part of.

If the result of this election isn't known definitively by June 30, 2027, 11:59 PM ET, the market will resolve to "Other".

This market will resolve based on the result of the election, as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the Brazilian government, specifically the Superior Electoral Court (Tribunal Superior Eleitoral, TSE) (e.g., www.tse.jus.br/eleicoes/resultados-eleicoes).

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus heavily favors Partido Liberal (PL) at 82.5% implied probability to win the most seats in Brazil's October 4, 2026, Senate election, where 27 seats—one per state and the Federal District—are at stake. This positioning stems from PL's recent ascent to the Senate's largest bancada with 15 senators following January switches and the April 2026 party affiliation window, bolstering its organizational strength. Aggregate state polls, including March Paraná Pesquisas and Real Time Big Data data compiled by VEJA, show PL candidates like Claudio Castro in Rio de Janeiro, Carlos Bolsonaro in Santa Catarina, and Michelle Bolsonaro in the DF leading or competitive in key races. Momentum from Senator Flávio Bolsonaro's tight presidential polls against Lula provides coattails, while UNIÃO, MDB, and PSD trail with leads in fewer states amid fragmented opposition. Upcoming candidate officializations and regional primaries could shift dynamics.

markets.question: Will the Democratic Party win the NY-13 House seat?
markets.description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the NY-13 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus heavily favors the Democratic Party at 93.5% to win New York's 13th congressional district House seat, reflecting its D+32 Cook Partisan Voter Index—the 11th most Democratic nationwide—and historical blowouts, including incumbent Adriano Espaillat's 83% victory in 2024. A March 2026 Upswing Research poll shows Espaillat leading the June 23 Democratic primary at 42% against challenger Darializa Avila Chevalier (28%) amid a crowded field, with his fundraising dominance ($1.1 million cash-on-hand). No Republican candidates have filed, underscoring the lack of viable GOP opposition. Upsets would require a high-profile Republican recruit, a scandal-plagued Democratic nominee, or an extraordinary national midterm wave.

markets.question: Fed Rate Hike by June 2026 Meeting?
markets.description:
This market will resolve to “Yes” if the upper bound of the target federal funds rate is increased at any point between December 16, 2025 and the completion of the listed Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting (inclusive of any rate hike announced as a result of the listed meeting). Otherwise, this market will resolve to “No”.

If the listed meeting does not take place within 7 calendar days (ET) of its scheduled end date, 11:59 PM ET, and no qualifying rate cut has been announced, this market will resolve to "No".

Emergency rate hikes will qualify.

The primary resolution source for this market will be the official website of the Federal Reserve (https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/openmarket.htm), however a consensus of credible reporting may also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus prices near-zero probability of a Federal Reserve rate hike at the April 28-29, 2026 FOMC meeting, with CME FedWatch implying over 98% odds of holding the federal funds target at 3.50%-3.75%, reflecting the Committee's March dot plot signaling one cut later in 2026 despite elevated uncertainty. March CPI surged to 3.3% annually—the highest since May 2024—on a 1.1% monthly jump, alongside 178,000 nonfarm payroll additions, stoking inflation fears and geopolitical risks from the Iran conflict that have nudged full-year 2026 hike odds higher to around 13% in prediction markets. Key watchpoints include the April FOMC statement and Powell's press conference, plus April CPI on May 12.

markets.question: Will the Fed’s lower bound reach 1.25% or lower before 2027?
markets.description:
The FED interest rates are defined in this market by the lower or the upper bound of the target federal funds range. The decisions on the target federal fund range are made by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meetings.

This market will resolve to “Yes” if the lower or the upper bound of the target federal funds rate reaches the specified level at any point by December 31, 2026, 12:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to “No.”

Emergency rate cuts and hikes outside the regularly scheduled meetings will be considered.

The resolution source for this market is the official website of the Federal Reserve at:
https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/openmarket.htm.

This market may resolve as soon as the relevant data showing the reached level is published.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket reflects a steady federal funds target range of 3.50%-3.75%, with the effective rate at 3.64% as of April 2026, amid resilient labor markets showing unemployment dipping to 4.3% in March and surging March CPI inflation at 3.3% year-over-year—driven by gasoline prices—halting near-term easing expectations. The March FOMC dot plot projects one 25-basis-point cut in 2026, targeting 3.00%-3.25% by end-2027, aligning with CME FedWatch probabilities of over 95% for no change at the April 28-29 meeting. Key catalysts ahead include April CPI on May 10 and June FOMC, where persistent inflation above 3% could delay cuts or prompt reassessment of the policy path.
